title: List Methods
beginnerExplanation: Lists come with powerful built-in methods that let you add, remove, search, sort, and transform items. These methods are the core toolkit for manipulating ordered collections in Python — append(), pop(), insert(), remove(), sort(), reverse(), index(), count(), extend(), and clear().
intermediateExplanation: Knowing how to create a list is step one. But in real programs, you constantly need to add items dynamically, remove duplicates, sort leaderboards, search for specific values, and merge multiple lists. List methods make all of this trivial.
advancedExplanation:
my_list.append(item)      # Add to end
my_list.insert(i, item)   # Add at index i
my_list.pop()             # Remove & return last
my_list.pop(i)            # Remove & return at index i
my_list.remove(item)      # Remove first match
my_list.sort()            # Sort in place
my_list.reverse()         # Reverse in place
my_list.index(item)       # Find index of item
my_list.count(item)       # Count occurrences
my_list.extend(other)     # Merge another list
my_list.clear()           # Remove all items

realWorldAnalogy.title: A To-Do List App
realWorldAnalogy.description: Your to-do list has features: add a task (append), insert a task at the top (insert), check off and remove (pop/remove), rearrange by priority (sort), see how many tasks you have (len), and find where a specific task is (index).

mentalModel: Your to-do list has features: add a task (append), insert a task at the top (insert), check off and remove (pop/remove), rearrange by priority (sort), see how many tasks you have (len), and find where a specific task is (index).
visualDiagram:
graph TD

  Process --> End
executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Writing new_list = my_list.sort() — .sort() returns None! Use sorted() instead.
Using .remove() on an item that does not exist causes a ValueError. Check first with "in".
Confusing .append([1,2]) (adds a list as one item) with .extend([1,2]) (adds each item).
Modifying a list while iterating over it — create a copy first with list() or [:].

debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Use .append() to add single items, .extend() to merge multiple items from another list.

practiceQuestions.question: What does my_list.sort() return?
practiceQuestions.explanation: .sort() modifies the list in place and returns None. If you want a new sorted list without changing the original, use sorted(my_list).

practiceQuestions.question: What is the difference between .append([1,2]) and .extend([1,2])?
options: append adds [1,2] as one item; extend adds 1 and 2 as separate items
extend adds [1,2] as one item; append adds separately
append throws an error with lists
practiceQuestions.explanation: .append() adds its argument as a single element: [[1,2]]. .extend() unpacks the iterable and adds each element individually: [1, 2].

practiceQuestions.question: What happens when you call .remove("x") and "x" is not in the list?
practiceQuestions.explanation: .remove() raises a ValueError if the item is not found. Always check with "if x in list:" before removing.

challengeQuestions.question: What is the correct keyword or operator most commonly associated with List Methods?
hints: Look at the syntax summary of the topic.
Check standard keyword usage in Python.

bossBattle.name: The Queue Master
bossBattle.description: A deranged librarian who constantly shuffles, inserts, and removes books from a magical shelf. You must use the correct list method for each operation to restore order.
bossBattle.mechanic: Given a target list state, use the minimum number of list method calls to transform a scrambled list into the correct order.

artifacts.name: The Sorting Hat
artifacts.description: A hat that can sort any list in O(n log n) time with a single command.

retentionSystem.revision1: Define the core syntax of List Methods.
retentionSystem.revision2: Name one common mistake when using List Methods.

developerMode.industryUsage: List Methods is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Use .append() to add single items, .extend() to merge multiple items from another list.
Use .pop() to remove and return the last item — great for stack-like behavior.
Remember that .sort() and .reverse() modify the list IN PLACE and return None.
Use sorted(list) if you want a new sorted list without changing the original.
antiPatterns: Writing new_list = my_list.sort() — .sort() returns None! Use sorted() instead.
Using .remove() on an item that does not exist causes a ValueError. Check first with "in".
Confusing .append([1,2]) (adds a list as one item) with .extend([1,2]) (adds each item).
Modifying a list while iterating over it — create a copy first with list() or [:].
developerMode.architectureNotes: Interpreted and executed in the primary application call loop.
